hook: A gem-studded catacomb skeleton in a glass case at St Peter's, holding a vial of her own dried blood.
facts: The relics of Saint Munditia are displayed in a glass coffin in a side altar of St Peter's Church (Peterskirche), the oldest parish church in Munich.
The skeleton was translated to Munich from the Roman catacombs of Cyriaca in 1675, and its Baroque shrine was consecrated on 5 September 1677.
The bones are sewn into a transparent gold-and-jewel-covered body stocking, with glass eyes set into the skull and the arms and legs adorned with alternating red and green jewels.
In 1804 the relics were concealed behind a wooden cover; this was removed in 1883, restoring public veneration.
In her hand the figure holds a glass container said to hold dried blood, a relic of her martyrdom.
St Peter's Church is open to visitors and its tower ('Alter Peter') can be climbed.
legend: Munditia is said to have been martyred around 310 AD, beheaded with a hatchet.
She is venerated as a patron saint of single women and spinsters, and her feast day is marked with a High Mass and a candlelit procession.
w: In a side chapel of St Peter's reclines Saint Munditia, a Roman catacomb martyr whose skeleton came to Munich in 1675, lavishly decked in gold, gems and glass eyes. She clutches a vial said to hold her dried blood. Venerated as patron of single women, she gazes from her case in unsettling splendour. Death, dressed to the nines.

hook: A tiny, dazzlingly ornate Late Baroque church the Asam brothers built as their own private chapel, squeezed between townhouses.
facts: St Johann Nepomuk, known as the Asamkirche, was built from 1733 to 1746 by the brothers Egid Quirin Asam (sculptor) and Cosmas Damian Asam (painter) as their private church.
It stands at Sendlinger Straße 32 in Munich's Altstadt, integrated into the row of terraced houses with a convex Baroque facade.
The two-storey interior measures only about 22 by 8 metres.
The ceiling fresco depicting the Life of Saint Nepomuk is regarded as a masterpiece of Cosmas Damian Asam.
Due to public pressure, the brothers were obliged to open the privately funded church to the public; it remains a working church open to visitors today.
It is considered one of the most important buildings of the southern German Late Baroque.
w: The Asam brothers built this jewel-box church beside their own house in the 1730s, a private chapel they could shape exactly as they pleased — and they pleased to excess. Barely wider than its altar, it crams in writhing gold, painted heavens and skeletons gripping the hour of death. Baroque, with the dial turned to eleven.

hook: A copper lindworm winding up the southwest corner of the New Town Hall, marking the dragon that legend says brought plague to Munich.
facts: The Wurmeck is the southwest corner of the Neues Rathaus (New Town Hall) on Marienplatz, where Weinstraße meets the square.
The town hall was completed in 1906; the corner turret carries reliefs across several levels depicting the Munich lindworm legend.
The copper dragon figure was modelled by sculptor Anton Kaindl and cast in copper by Hygin Kiene.
The name 'Wurmeck' derives from a medieval house (Marienplatz 3) recorded under that name since at least 1431, possibly referring to the coat of arms of the Schönecker owner family.
Reliefs at the corner show the lindworm driving away women and children, men preparing for battle, and the Schäffler (barrel-makers') victory dance.
legend: Local legend holds that in the Middle Ages a winged serpent crept out of the ground in central Munich and spread the Black Death, until brave citizens fought and killed it.
The barrel-makers' Schäffler dance, performed in the town hall's lower carillon, is tied to celebrating the city's deliverance from the plague.
w: A scaly stone dragon clambers up the south-west corner of the New Town Hall, claws out, jaws wide. The Wurmeck recalls a medieval legend that a basilisk's breath once brought plague to Munich. Carved around 1908 when the hall went up, it lurks above the Marienplatz crowds. The city's pet monster, in stone.

hook: A 1904 Art Nouveau fountain of Little Red Riding Hood and the wolf, with four wolf heads spouting water from their howling jaws.
facts: The Wolfsbrunnen is an Art Nouveau fountain inaugurated in 1904, designed by sculptors Heinrich Düll and Georg Pezold.
It stands in the middle of Am Kosttor, near Maximilianstraße in Munich's Altstadt.
The sculpture depicts a young girl (Little Red Riding Hood / Rotkäppchen) pressed against the side of a wolf, the two peering at each other.
At the base of the pillar are four wolf heads from which water spouts into a basin below.
It was commissioned by the wealthy benefactor couple Adolph and Apollonia Wolf to memorialise their family name, punning on 'Wolf'.
w: Tucked off Maximilianstrasse stands a charming 1904 fountain telling a darker fairy tale: Little Red Riding Hood, basket in hand, beside the sly wolf, water spouting from snarling wolf-head mouths. A piece of Art Nouveau whimsy that most hurrying locals never notice. Find it near the old Kosttor. Grimm, cast in bronze and stone.

hook: A restored 1968 flying-saucer plastic house, one of fewer than 100 ever made, parked on the lawn beside the Pinakothek der Moderne.
facts: The Futuro is a UFO-shaped prefabricated plastic house designed by Finnish architect Matti Suuronen (1965–1968), originally conceived as a ski cabin.
It stands on the outdoor lawn beside the Pinakothek der Moderne in Munich's Maxvorstadt/Kunstareal.
The ellipsoid shell, about 8 m in diameter and just under 6 m tall, rests on eight legs and offers around 25 m² of interior space.
It is built from 16 fibreglass-reinforced polyester arc segments that can be assembled or dismantled in about two days.
It was acquired in 2016 for Die Neue Sammlung – The Design Museum, comprehensively restored, and first shown to the public in 2017–2018.
The exterior remains on view, but the interior has been closed to visitors since the COVID-19 pandemic.
w: A fibreglass flying saucer has landed on a museum lawn in Maxvorstadt: a Futuro House, the prefab plastic pod designed by Finn Matti Suuronen in 1968 as the holiday home of the space age. Only a few dozen were made. Restored beside the Pinakothek der Moderne, it gleams like a future that never arrived. Retro-tomorrow, in plastic.

hook: Olafur Eliasson's double-helix staircase that loops endlessly with no destination, hidden in the courtyard of a KPMG office.
facts: Umschreibung is a sculpture by Olafur Eliasson completed in 2004 in the courtyard of the KPMG headquarters in Munich.
It consists of two interlocking spiral staircases forming a continuous double-helix loop, about 9 m (30 ft) tall and made of steel.
To design it, a double helix was projected onto the surface of a sphere, with step heights varying to compensate for the curvature so the structure balances on a single point.
The artist describes it as 'movement without destination, a space defined by motion rather than walls'.
It sits in the courtyard at Ganghoferstraße 29A in the Schwanthalerhöhe (Westend) district, a short walk from the Schwanthalerhöhe U-Bahn station.
Access is via a private/semi-public office courtyard rather than open public space.
w: In a corporate courtyard off Ganghoferstrasse, Olafur Eliasson set a double-helix staircase spiralling up and folding back on itself — a stair that climbs to nowhere and everywhere at once. 'Umschreibung' lets you ascend one helix and descend the other in an endless loop. A philosophical riddle you can actually climb.

hook: Shipping containers, retired Munich U-Bahn cars and an old railbus welded into one sprawling techno-and-culture playground on the former cattle-yard rail sidings.
facts: Founded in 2015 on the grounds of the old Viehhof; moved about 400 m to the former Viehbahnhof (freight station) site in late 2017.
The venue is built from repurposed intermodal shipping containers combined with decommissioned Munich U-Bahn carriages and a vintage 1967 tram from Ulm (added 2019).
Started life around a repurposed MAN railbus before expanding with containers and transit vehicles into a multifunctional cultural space.
Programming spans theatre, concerts, dance school, live radio, station cinema and readings during the week, turning into a techno club with name DJs at weekends.
w: On the old Viehhof slaughterhouse sidings, stacked shipping containers and decommissioned U-Bahn carriages have grown into Munich's wildest cultural playground — graffiti, hammocks, flea markets, art and techno till dawn. Bahnwarter Thiel takes its name from a Hauptmann novella, its spirit from Berlin. Subculture on a dead rail yard.

hook: A 1950s Ammersee lake steamer hoisted onto a disused railway bridge above the street, now a bar, restaurant and engine-room disco.
facts: The 36.4 m, 144-ton vessel was built in 1949 by Deggendorfer Werft und Eisenbau and ran as the MS Utting passenger ferry on the Ammersee from 1950 to 2016.
It was transported to Munich on 21–22 February 2017, requiring an Autobahn closure, and set on a former Isartalbahn railway bridge over Lagerhausstraße.
After structural conversion it opened as a venue on 26 July 2018, run by Kulturverein Wannda e.V. as bar, restaurant, discotheque (in the engine room), music venue and cabaret stage.
Address: Lagerhausstraße 15, 81371 Munich.
w: A retired Ammersee lake steamer sits marooned thirty feet up on a disused railway bridge over Sendling, hauled there by crane in 2018 to become a bar, kitchen and club. You climb aboard the MS Utting for drinks on deck and dancing below. A ship that will never sail again, going nowhere in the best way. All aboard the bridge.

hook: A creative quarter of stacked containers, clubs and art studios on former industrial land by Ostbahnhof, crowned by the giant Umadum wheel that packs into 80 shipping containers.
facts: Built on the former Pfanni potato-factory and industrial site beside Munich's Ostbahnhof, the district mixes container architecture, nightlife, studios and the future Munich Concert Hall.
The Umadum (formerly Hi-Sky) Ferris wheel opened in April 2019 and stands 78 m tall with 27 gondolas seating up to sixteen people each.
Umadum is listed in the Guinness Book of Records as the world's largest transportable Ferris wheel, fitting into 80 shipping containers for transport.
Address: Atelierstraße 11, Werksviertel-Mitte, 81671 Munich; served by U5 and S-Bahn at Ostbahnhof.
w: Where the Pfanni dumpling factory once stood by the Ostbahnhof, a whole creative quarter has risen from shipping containers, old halls and a giant Ferris wheel called Umadum. Clubs, studios, street food, a climbing hall and rooftop sheep share the Werksviertel. Forever half-finished and the better for it. Industrial Munich, reinvented.

hook: The 1972 women's Olympic Village turned student bungalow colony, where residents have for decades painted their concrete huts in a riot of murals and slogans.
facts: The single-storey bungalows housed female athletes at the 1972 Summer Olympics and were designed in part by architect Werner Wirsing (with Günther Eckert).
Today they form the Studentenviertel/Studentendorf Oberwiesenfeld, run by Studierendenwerk München, the city's second-largest student estate after Studentenstadt Freimann.
The painted front doors and facades are described as an expression of European youth culture; new residents have the right to design their own bungalow facade.
A 2007–2010 reconstruction (with heritage authorities) rebuilt the bungalows, narrowing them from 4.20 m to 3.15 m and raising the count from about 800 to 1,052 units.
w: The single-storey bungalows that housed female athletes at the 1972 Olympics now belong to students, who have painted every wall in a riot of murals and colour over five decades. The Olympic Village's pedestrian decks and terraces hum with alternative life. Wander between the painted huts. Munich's most psychedelic postcode.

hook: A laid-back lakeside café on the Mollsee in Westpark where you sip your coffee perched in a sawn-off bathtub or on a stack of Euro pallets.
facts: It is a Bauwagen (construction-trailer) café set directly on the bank of the Mollsee lake in Westpark.
Seating is made from bathtubs and furniture built out of Euro pallets, with antique pieces alongside.
The menu includes homemade rosemary fries, a vegan/vegetarian seitan döner, a fair-weather grill stand and homemade cakes.
It was founded by the operators behind Bahnwärter Thiel and runs a subcultural programme of concerts, cabaret, improv theatre and readings.
Location: Siegenburger Straße / Hansapark, Sendling-Westpark, beside the Mollsee.
w: A scruffy-charming cafe in a converted builder's caravan on the shore of Westpark's Mollsee, where you lounge in old bathtubs and on pallet sofas with a drink and the ducks. Gans am Wasser keeps the loose feel of a squat-turned-beach-bar, far from any polish. Grab a deckchair by the water and stay till dusk. Munich, with its shoes off.

hook: An ephemeral summer urban beach of trucked-in sand, deckchairs and DJ sets that wraps around the Vater-Rhein-Brunnen on the Isar island by the Ludwigsbrücke.
facts: The M-net Kulturstrand sets up each summer around the Vater-Rhein-Brunnen on the Museumsinsel/Praterinsel, directly by the Ludwigsbrücke near the Deutsches Museum.
The 2026 edition runs 1 May to 14 September, with free admission and daily opening (in good weather) roughly 12:00–23:00.
It offers a daily-changing programme of live music, DJ sets, children's activities, a sports area, flea market, beach bar and food stalls.
A second, related site, the Maximiliansstrand, sets up at the Wittelsbacherbrunnen on Maximiliansplatz.
The Vater-Rhein-Brunnen fountain itself sits on the Museumsinsel just north of the Ludwigsbrücke, reached past the Wehrsteg footbridge to the Praterinsel.
w: Each summer a stretch of the Isar island sprouts an instant beach: sand, deckchairs, palms, drinks and DJ sets beside the Vater-Rhein fountain, built by a volunteer collective and gone again by autumn. Kulturstrand has been Munich's pop-up riverside living room since 2010, free to wander into. Here today, gone by the frost.

hook: A wrought-iron 1890s railway bridge where Müncheners gather on the steel girders with drinks to watch the trains fan out below and the sun set over the tracks.
facts: The riveted iron bridge was built between 1890 and 1894 over the throat of tracks leading into Munich Hauptbahnhof.
It consists of six riveted arch girders, each about 28 m long and 8 m high, carrying a roadway roughly 7 m wide.
It links Landsberger Straße in the Westend to Arnulfstraße north of the main-station forecourt and the former Marsfeld in Maxvorstadt.
In summer it is one of the city's best-known sunset spots, with crowds sitting on the steel beams above the rail yard.
w: A graceful iron railway bridge from the 1890s arcs over the tracks west of the Hauptbahnhof, and as the sun drops behind the rails it fills with locals perched on the parapet with beers. Trains rumble out beneath your feet. Hackerbrucke has become Munich's unofficial sunset balcony. The city's best free seat at dusk.

hook: A wavy line of bronze cobbles marks the back-alley shortcut Müncheners used to dodge the obligatory Nazi salute at the Feldherrnhalle.
facts: Officially named after Swiss Baroque architect Giovanni Antonio Viscardi; the nickname Drückebergergasse means 'shirkers' alley'.
Connects Residenzstrasse/Theatinerstrasse just west of the Feldherrnhalle on Odeonsplatz, the site of a Nazi memorial to the 1923 Beer Hall Putsch.
From 1933, an SS guard at the Feldherrnhalle required passers-by to give the Hitler salute; people took this short detour to avoid it.
A roughly 30-cm-wide winding bronze trail was set into the pavement in 1995 to commemorate this quiet act of evasion.
The pedestrian lane is a little over 50 metres long and paved with stone setts throughout.
w: A line of golden cobbles snakes down this little alley behind the Feldherrnhalle, tracing the route Munchners took to avoid the SS guards who demanded a Hitler salute at the monument. So many slipped down here that it earned the nickname Druckebergergasse — 'shirkers' lane.' Quiet defiance, paved in gold.

hook: A moated late-Gothic ducal hunting lodge in Munich's western edge that now holds the world's largest library of children's books.
facts: A moated castle on the site dates to the 13th century; the present complex with its chapel is largely 15th-century, built as a ducal hunting lodge.
Since 1983 it has housed the Internationale Jugendbibliothek (International Youth Library), founded by Jella Lepman in 1949.
The library holds over 500,000 volumes and is among the largest of its kind worldwide.
The castle sits in the Obermenzing quarter on the western edge of Munich, surrounded by a moat fed from the Würm.
Its late-Gothic chapel (Schlosskapelle) retains original 15th-century stained glass and winged altars.
w: A small moated castle on the western edge of the city, built as a ducal hunting lodge in the 1400s and never fortified, only enjoyed. Today it holds the International Youth Library, the world's largest collection of children's books — a literal 'book castle' with a Gothic chapel and river walks. Fairy tales, in their natural habitat.

hook: A luminous glass cube containing a wooden cube containing the church, fronted by what are claimed to be the largest church doors in the world.
facts: The present church was built 1997-2000 to a design by Munich architects Allmann Sattler Wappner, after a fire destroyed the previous building in November 1994.
It is a Catholic church at Lachnerstrasse 8 in the Neuhausen-Nymphenburg borough.
A 16-metre-high glass front forms a pair of double-winged doors that can open the entire facade, reportedly the largest set of church doors in the world.
The blue glass facade is etched with a symbolic alphabet by artist Alexander Beleschenko spelling passages from the St John Passion.
Inside the outer glass cube stands a second, wooden cube of maple slats that forms the actual worship space.
Cardinal Friedrich Wetter consecrated the building on 26 November 2000.
w: From outside, a giant glass cube; inside, the largest church doors in the world swing open and light pours through a skin of deep blue glass that pales toward the altar. Herz-Jesu, finished in 2000, reinvented the Catholic church as a luminous box, a timber shell glowing within the crystal one. Modern faith, built from light.

hook: Munich's main synagogue: a travertine stone base evoking the Western Wall topped by a glass-and-bronze-mesh lantern, opened on the anniversary of Kristallnacht.
facts: The synagogue stands on Sankt-Jakobs-Platz in the Altstadt as part of the new Jewish Centre, which also includes the Jewish Museum and community centre.
It was built 2004-2006 and inaugurated on 9 November 2006, the 68th anniversary of the 1938 Kristallnacht pogrom.
Designed by architects Rena Wandel-Hoefer and Wolfgang Lorch, who had earlier built the New Synagogue in Dresden.
The solid travertine stone base recalls the Temple in Jerusalem; an upper glass cube wrapped in a bronze metal mesh forms a 'tent' (the name Ohel Jakob means 'Tent of Jacob').
An underground 'Corridor of Memory' links the synagogue to the community building, lined with names of Munich Jews murdered in the Holocaust.
w: Munich's main synagogue rises on Sankt-Jakobs-Platz as a rough travertine base — evoking the Western Wall — topped by a glowing glass-and-metal tent, like the tabernacle carried through the desert. Opened in 2006, sixty-eight years after Kristallnacht, it stands as a deliberate statement of return. Renewal, in stone and light.

hook: A monumental Nazi-era temple of 'German art', built for Hitler beside the English Garden and now a leading contemporary-art kunsthalle.
facts: Built 1933-1937 to a monumental neo-classical design by Paul Ludwig Troost as the Haus der Deutschen Kunst, commissioned by Adolf Hitler.
It was the first major Nazi monumental building and opened in 1937 with the 'Great German Art Exhibition', staged opposite the 'Degenerate Art' show.
It stands on Prinzregentenstrasse 1 at the southern edge of the Englischer Garten in the Lehel district.
Today it is a non-collecting institution presenting changing exhibitions of modern and contemporary art.
Its long colonnaded portico of square pillars is a surviving example of Nazi-era 'Troost-style' architecture.
w: The Nazis' first monumental building, this stripped-classical colonnade opened in 1937 to show 'great German art,' across town from their notorious 'Degenerate Art' show. Today the same forbidding halls host cutting-edge contemporary exhibitions, architecture and art in pointed tension. History, on the walls and in them.

hook: A neo-baroque Palace of Justice crowned by a vast iron-and-glass dome, where the Nazi People's Court condemned the White Rose resisters.
facts: Built 1890-1897 by architect Friedrich von Thiersch in a Neo-Baroque style, on the north-west side of Karlsplatz (Stachus).
The central iron-and-glass dome rises to roughly 67 metres above the atrium, spanning about 19 by 29 metres.
The building is about 138 metres long and houses the Bavarian Ministry of Justice and civil chambers of the Munich I Regional Court.
In February 1943 the Nazi People's Court (Volksgerichtshof) sentenced members of the White Rose resistance group to death here.
Thiersch later added the adjacent 'New Justizpalast' (1905) in red-brick Brick Gothic style to contrast with the original.
w: Behind a wedding-cake neo-baroque facade at Stachus hides a breathtaking glass-and-iron dome, flooding the great stairhall with light. The Palace of Justice has dispensed Bavarian law since 1897 — and in one room the White Rose students were condemned to death in 1943. Grand justice, and a grave memory, under one roof.

hook: A Renaissance four-wing courtyard built as ducal stables and art galleries, later the royal mint, hiding three tiers of arcades off Hofgraben.
facts: Built 1563-1567 by court architect Wilhelm Egckl (design attributed to Bernhard Zwitzel of Augsburg) for the stables and art collections of Duke Albert V of Bavaria.
Its inner courtyard preserves an authentic three-tiered Renaissance arcade with sturdy columns and wide arches, unusual for the period.
From the early 19th century it served as the royal Bavarian mint, giving it the name Alte Münze (Old Mint).
The west facade on Pfisterstrasse was redesigned in Neoclassical style in 1809.
It now houses the Bavarian State Office for Monument Preservation (Bayerisches Landesamt für Denkmalpflege).
w: Step off a busy old-town street into the hidden courtyard of the Alte Munze, three storeys of Renaissance arcades around a quiet square. Built in the 1560s as the duke's stables and art chamber, it later struck the kingdom's coins for over a century. Few tourists find the gateway. The mint that minted Bavaria.

hook: An underground platform plunged into blue gloom and lit by eleven giant glowing domes in red, blue and yellow by lighting designer Ingo Maurer.
facts: A station on the U1 line of the Munich U-Bahn, opened on 24 May 1998.
The station architecture is by Auer Weber; the lighting was added in 2001 by designer Ingo Maurer (1932-2019).
Eleven deeply suspended dome lamps, each 3.8 metres in diameter, glow in blue, red and yellow above the platform.
The rough, rock-like sprayed-concrete tunnel walls were deliberately left exposed and bathed in subdued blue light.
It lies on Orpheusstrasse in the Neuhausen-Nymphenburg district, named for the adjacent Westfriedhof (West Cemetery).
w: Descend into Westfriedhof station and the platform glows under eleven huge dome lamps, three metres across, washing the concrete in pools of blue, red and yellow. Lighting designer Ingo Maurer turned a 1998 metro stop into a subterranean light installation. Ride out to stand on the platform. Munich's loveliest place to wait for a train.

hook: Man-made standing river wave where wetsuited surfers queue to ride a side-arm of the Isar year-round, right under the Haus der Kunst.
facts: Located on the Eisbach, a side branch of the Isar, at the southern tip of the English Garden, just downstream of the bridge opposite Prinzregentenstrasse 22 next to the Haus der Kunst.

A stone step at the channel outlet creates a permanent standing wave roughly half a metre to about one metre high; the water is cold and shallow, sometimes only about 40 cm deep, so it suits only experienced surfers.
Munich is considered the birthplace of river surfing, and the Eisbach is its largest and most consistent central-city wave; surfing it was formally legalised by the city in 2010.

In early November 2025 the wave vanished after a city dredging project removed sediment from the channel bed; after intervention and adjustment it naturally re-formed and was officially reopened for surfing.
w: At the south tip of the English Garden, a standing wave rears up where the Eisbach surges under a bridge, and surfers queue on the bank to drop in — all year, wetsuited through the snow. Once an illegal secret, the Eisbachwelle is now Munich's strangest landmark, watched by crowds from the rails. River surfing, far from any sea.

hook: Concrete bridge piers standing in the Isar riverbed, repainted by graffiti artists every year as one of Munich's few legal street-art walls.
facts: The piers of the Brudermühlbrücke, where the bridge crosses the Isar at the northern edge of the Flaucher, are one of the few legal graffiti locations in Munich.
Since 1996 the pillars on the east side have been freshly sprayed each year as part of the 'isart' project run by the municipal youth-culture institution Die Faerberei.
The painted piers sit directly in the river / gravel bed and are visible from a distance along the Isar riverside paths.
w: Beneath the Brudermuhl bridge, the concrete piers along the Isar have served as a legal graffiti canvas since 1996, repainted top to bottom each year by local spray-can artists. The riverside 'isart' wall turns a grey underpass into an ever-changing gallery. Cycle the Isar path and watch it shift. Street art with the council's blessing.

hook: An ensemble of Asian temple architecture — a gilded Thai sala, a hand-carved Nepalese pagoda and Chinese and Japanese gardens — left over from a 1983 garden show.
facts: The East Asia ensemble in the western part of Westpark was created for the International Garden Exhibition (Internationale Gartenbauausstellung, IGA) of 1983.
The Nepalese pagoda was carved in Nepal by roughly 300 craftsmen over about seven months, modelled on the Pashupatinath temple, and reassembled in the park; it weighs around 80 tonnes.
The Thai sala, built in Bangkok style, houses a 3.5-metre gilded Buddha carved from a maple trunk by Noppadol Khamlae in 1994 and consecrated as the first free-standing Buddhist sanctuary in Germany.
The ensemble also includes a Chinese garden and a Japanese garden grouped near the Thai sala.
w: Left over from a 1983 garden show, a cluster of authentic Asian buildings hides in the lawns of Westpark: a gilded Thai sala, a carved Nepalese pagoda, and Chinese and Japanese garden corners by the lakes. Far from the centre and rarely crowded, they reward a slow wander. East Asia, transplanted to suburban Munich.

hook: Wild stretch of the Isar with gravel banks and side channels where Munich comes to barbecue, sunbathe nude and swim in clear water.
facts: The Flaucher is a near-natural section of the Isar floodplain in the south of Sendling on the border with Thalkirchen, with the river splitting into arms and gravel islands.
Bathing, nudism and open-fire barbecuing are all legally permitted here, which is unusual for Munich's public spaces.
It lies immediately next to Hellabrunn Zoo and is one of the city's most popular summer recreation areas; the named beer garden 'Zum Flaucher' sits on the site.
The name derives from a 19th-century innkeeper; just downstream the Isar drops over the Thalkirchner Ueberfaelle (weir).
w: Where the Isar braids into gravel islands south of the centre, the Flaucher is Munich's wild back garden: locals swim the cold green channels, grill sausages on the stony banks and sprawl in the sun all summer. Almost natural, barely managed, gloriously informal. The city's favourite stretch of untamed water.

hook: The canal landing where traditional Bavarian log rafts, after a ~30 km river journey, finally come ashore in the city.
facts: The Floßlände (Zentrallände) in Munich-Thalkirchen is the terminus where commercial Isar log-raft trips (Floßfahrten) end after a roughly 30 km journey down the Isar valley.
Rafts typically arrive in the afternoon, around 16:00-16:30; the landing lies beside the raft canal that branches off the Isar-Werkkanal, near the U3/U6 Thalkirchen U-Bahn station.
The same artificial Floßkanal hosts a smaller surfable standing wave (the Floßlände wave), used by river surfers when water is released.
w: After drifting down from the foothills, the great Bavarian log rafts — beer-soaked, brass-band parties on bound timber — come ashore at the Thalkirchen Flosslande, the end of a tradition centuries old. The same channel hosts a tamer surf wave for beginners. Catch a raft landing in summer. Where the river journey ends.

hook: Bronze leaflets and farewell letters set flush into the pavement outside the university, marking where the White Rose students scattered their anti-Nazi tracts.
facts: The ground memorial sits on Geschwister-Scholl-Platz in front of the main entrance of the Ludwig-Maximilians-Universitaet (LMU), the square renamed for siblings Hans and Sophie Scholl in 1946.

Created by artist Robert Schmidt-Matt and installed in 1988, it reproduces, in bronze cast into the paving, the White Rose leaflets, portrait photos of the executed members and Willi Graf's farewell letter.

On 18 February 1943 Hans and Sophie Scholl threw the group's sixth leaflet from the upper gallery of the LMU light-court (Lichthof); they were arrested, and they and Christoph Probst were executed days later.
The memorial spreads across the paving toward Ludwigstrasse and is complemented by the DenkStaette Weisse Rose exhibition inside the LMU.
w: Scattered across the pavement outside the university, bronze casts of leaflets and a portrait lie as if just dropped — the memorial to the White Rose, the students who flung anti-Nazi pamphlets here until the Gestapo caught and beheaded them in 1943. Sophie Scholl threw her last leaflets from the atrium above. Courage, set in the ground.

hook: A century-old, larch-clad footbridge that strides over the Isar's weirs and gravel islands, linking three districts at the south end of the Flaucher.
facts: The Flauchersteg is a pedestrian bridge crossing the Isar at the southern end of the Flaucher, spanning the Thalkirchner Ueberfaelle weir where the river drops about 2.5 metres over several gravel islands.

First built as a wooden bridge on concrete piers in 1914, its timber framework was replaced in 2000/2001 by a steel structure clad in larch wood to keep its original character; it is about 340 m long.

Its northern third lies in Sendling and the southern two-thirds in Thalkirchen, ending in the Siebenbrunn district of Untergiesing-Harlaching, so the single bridge connects three Munich neighbourhoods.
It lies roughly 400 m southwest of the main Flaucher barbecue/swimming spot, so it is treated as a distinct landmark rather than a duplicate.
w: A handsome larch-clad footbridge arcs over the Isar weir at the Flaucher, more than a century old and beloved by walkers, swimmers and sunset-watchers. From its planks you look down on the rushing water and the gravel-bank bathers below. Less a crossing than a place to linger. The Flaucher's wooden heart.

hook: Munich's beer and Oktoberfest history packed into one of the city's oldest townhouses, all wonky floors, beams and a former 'black kitchen'.
facts: The museum is at Sterneckerstrasse 2 in the Altstadt and is housed in one of Munich's oldest surviving townhouses, with origins dating to about 1340, built two decades after the city fire of 1327.

Its beer room, the former cellar, still shows massive fireproof brick walls with river-pebble layers and oak beams from the first half of the 14th century, plus a ceiling painting dated 1340 and a 'schwarze Kuchl' (black kitchen).
Exhibits trace the history of Bavarian beer and brewing and the story of the Oktoberfest from its 1810 origins.
w: Munich's beer story fills one of the old town's oldest houses, a leaning medieval townhouse of around 1340 with creaking stairs and low beams. The Beer and Oktoberfest Museum traces the city's brewing law, its monastery roots and the rise of the world's biggest folk festival, then pours you a stein in its tavern. Drink in the past.
